title: Enable Account Lockout
objective: Enable account lockout after repeated failed logins, with a meaningfully low attempt limit.
briefing: Logs show the {victim_username} account was compromised after repeated password guesses with no lockout in place. The login endpoint needs a failed-attempt limit.
hints: Restarting the web server doesn't add a lockout policy that doesn't exist yet.
The fix goes in the app's auth configuration, not the web server.
debrief: The patch enables a lockout policy: after a small number of failed attempts, the account is temporarily locked, which turns brute-forcing from 'guess forever' into 'guess a handful of times, then wait.' Setting the limit too high (hundreds or thousands of attempts) technically 'enables lockout' but doesn't meaningfully stop brute-forcing - the limit itself is part of the fix, not just the feature flag. This directly addresses Identification and Authentication Failures (A07:2021). For a production system you'd go further - rate limit by IP as well as account, and add MFA so a correct guess alone isn't sufficient.

logs: [AUTH] {victim_username} account compromised via brute force - no lockout in place

cases.output:
patched /etc/app/auth.conf
lockout policy: {max} failed attempts -> 15 min lock... done

logs_append: [FIX] account lockout enabled after {max} failed attempts

cases.output:
patched /etc/app/auth.conf
lockout policy: {max} failed attempts -> 15 min lock... done
WARNING: {max} attempts is too permissive to meaningfully slow down brute-forcing
